slides.title: Values On Demand, Not All At Once
slides.body: A **generator** is a function that hands you values **one at a time** — only when you ask. Instead of building a giant list in memory, it lazily produces each item on demand.
slides.tip: Think of it like a vending machine: one snack at a time, not the whole machine dumped on your lap.

slides.title: `yield` Pauses, Remembers, Resumes
slides.body: Replace `return` with `yield` and your function becomes a generator. Every time Python hits `yield`, it **pauses** and hands out that value — then **picks up right where it left off** on the next call.
slides.code:
def count_up(limit):
    n = 0
    while n < limit:
        yield n
        n += 1

gen = count_up(3)
print(next(gen))
print(next(gen))

slides.title: `for` Loops Do the Heavy Lifting
slides.body: You rarely call `next()` by hand. A **`for` loop** automatically pulls values one at a time until the generator is exhausted — clean and effortless.
slides.tip: Only five numbers ever live in memory at once — no matter how big `limit` gets.

slides.title: Generator Expressions: The Shortcut
slides.body: Swap `[]` for `()` on a list comprehension and you get a **generator expression** — same idea, zero upfront memory cost.
slides.code:
# List: all values built right now
list_ver = [x * x for x in range(5)]

# Generator: values built on demand
gen_ver = (x * x for x in range(5))
print(sum(gen_ver))
slides.tip: Pass generator expressions directly to `sum()`, `max()`, or `min()` — no list needed.

slides.title: Generators Can Go On Forever
slides.body: Because values are produced lazily, a generator can represent an **infinite sequence** — impossible with a plain list. Just stop asking when you have enough.
slides.code:
def count_forever(start=0):
    n = start
    while True:
        yield n
        n += 1

counter = count_forever(10)
print(next(counter))
print(next(counter))
slides.tip: Never do `list(infinite_generator)` — Python will try to collect endless values and freeze.

slides.title: You Get Generators Now!
slides.body: Use `yield` to make a function lazy, loop with `for` to consume it, and reach for generator expressions when you need a compact one-liner. Your programs just got lighter on memory.
slides.tip: One gotcha: a generator is exhausted after one full pass — create a fresh one if you need to iterate again.
